item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I live in the Northwest, and the legend of the Stick People has fascinated me for as long as I've lived here. This is a land where the cities are surrounded by ancient forests where one could easily get lost for good, where one could easily imagine all sorts of elder beings, to whom even the first humans to arrive here are merely newcomers, which one must always be conscious of because to even mention them aloud would be to attract their attention.

I like seeing this bit of local mythology presented on a wider stage, but I feel that it ultimately fails to do it justice.

No-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Affected trees and soil were left in advanced stages of dessication and malnourishment, and survived in only 27% of cases.

You either need to rephrase that or remove "soil", since right now it sounds like it means the soil becomes sickly and dies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Way, way better. Fits the Foundation's MO way better! I mean, personally I would like to examine those dissecated trees too, to learn how is the creature feeding, and who knows if that kind of wood has uses on itself? XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm… I honestly hadn't considered that, I guess I just jumped straight to putting it in a containment cube type room. I think I agree with you guys, and I'll make the changes you suggest.

Edit: Alright, I made the suggested changes. Please review and tell me what you think!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Exactly. As I understand it, the most accepted concept is that the Foundation HOARDS anomalies. They try their damned best to keep them alive unless there's no other option at all.
And this being the Foundation, I don't think it would be hard to transplant a tree in there once in a while!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No. Deliberately letting a creature starve to death is absolutely cruel, not cold, and no, the most accepted canon is not that "they decommission SCPs all the time". That's what the GOC does. The Foundation just keeps these things squirreled away forever in case they might be useful in the future.

There is literally no ethical reason this thing should not be in a secure enclosure with trees and being fed to the best of the Foundation's ability.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As per your suggestions, I have changed them to a MTF. thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded on changing it to MTF-{make up a unit number} ("Gag, Bag & Tag"). Its a continuity thing mostly, there's precious little everyone generally agrees on and all units being named following the MTF standard is one of them. Rather like that name for a unit specializing in capturing cryptids, on a side note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still have no idea what a GBT team is tho.

Although I already gave you a cute MTF name, MTF-Beta-34 ("Gag, Bag & Tag").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had thought about that, but I felt lke since this was just a small part of a summary report of acquisition, I would just use some shorthand, rather than come up with some cute MTF name and pull a Greek letter and number combination out of my ass. Doesn't really serve the story. If others feel like it should be a proper MTF I can change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The most accepted canon (lolnocanon) of the Foundation is 'cold, not cruel'. They won't harm it without purpose, but they decommission SCPs all the time rather than expend resources keeping it.alive and contained. This is just passively letting one die since it'll have outlived its usefulness by then. Not to mention they now have reason to believe it could be replaced if needed, and they probably will have more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it, but I have one issue: What the hell is a 'Gag, Bag & Tag' team? Just say they send a Mobile Task Force, I dunno, MTF-Beta-34 ("Gag, Bag & Tag"). It would be better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Oh, it'll die if we don't feed it? That's ok, we'll know all we need by then"

That, for me, goes against the usual MO of the Fundation. Don't they try to keep their objects alive and healthy whenever possible?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like I've seen this done before, or rather seen various parts of it (including the funnel-shaped mouth on the Chupassiah) in various other SCPs.

The one thing really bothering me is the use of Primatomorpha. Quick Googling reveals it to be a huge order, encompassing the entire line of primates (and thus us). It doesn't narrow down the thing's look, is what I'm saying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even though I'm sure there are others like it, I've never heard of a monster (Or an SCP) quite like this one. Interesting.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even though I'm sure there are plenty of things like it, I've never heard of a monster (or

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait really? Which one? And I've heard a lot about that salish tribe, now I feel like I have to investigate them lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this SCP. What's cool about it is not only the fact that it reacts to the environment, but the fact that when it starting "singing", attacks started becoming more frequent, almost giving it a wolf in a wolf pack feeling to it, and it gives you the feeling like they're trying to look for the missing one, which I think it makes one feel a tenseness that they will eventually find the missing one.
Good job, I enjoyed it.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2056 will also sporadically vocalize, appearing to 'sing' for minutes at a time before falling silent. The purpose of this behavior has not yet been determined, but is theorized to be in response to its extended incarceration.

;_;

I like this a lot. I heart wendigos of all kinds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fact that it's a dangerous object that in the past has (based on containment procedures) been violent is enough for them to keep guarding it. I don't see the Foundation as saying "It hasn't tried to escape in a while so we don't need to guard it anymore."

Edit: Also, hey, thanks for tagging this. Seriously. The tagging staffers don't get enough credit, as far as I ever see anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback! This is actually a salish tribe variation on the wendigo, but I altered it, because I think we DO have a wendigo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one. You use actual scientific language that appeals to my inner biologist, but you keep it to the level where people unfamiliar with science can understand it. I don't see a real big "hook," but I feel like you can take this a bit father. I hope you do, that last addendum sets this up for some nice stories.

Also, you got me excited when you mentioned the Native Americans in this, I thought we finally got a wendigo SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The cell is to be guarded at all times by no less than four security personnel armed with gas-powered firearms loaded with 10mg Etorphine cartridges.

Considering that now it's malnourished and isn't even trying to escape, maybe the containment procedures should be revised

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a whole lot.

Asides from the neat creature itself, I like how it's an organic part of its surroundings, including the local culture - after all, anomalies don't magically appear ten minutes before containment, but often are a part of the local enviroment for centuries.

+1

SCP-2056 is a bipedal humanoid, superficially resembling animals from the Primatomorpha order of mammals

SCP-2056 is a bipedal humanoid superficially resembling mammals of the Primatomorpha order.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of the Blair Witch Project and seemd just as scary to me. Upvoted

*drops camera and screams

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I recognized the image and was like "oh 343, it's back again…?" but then I read the rewrite and was happily surprised. Take an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback!

As far as the "like most monsters I guess" thing, I meant for him to say that in an informal kind of way. Kind of like "These things are like most monsters you've heard of; big and scary". I mean, everybody pictures some basic physical characteristics when they hear the word 'monster'.

I also want to mention here (Since I didn't want to make a separate post for it) that I included the image as a nod to the original author who clearly based much of his article around it. While I know that 'artist renderings' of skips are generally not well received unless it's part of the effect, in this case I feel it's acceptable given this skip's cultural/legend status.

EDIT: Well, the image was actually in the original author's sandbox, which I had still linked in this iteration. The image is now gone, and honestly I was on the fence about it, so it's gone and I think this stands without it, or is perhaps even improved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this.

I mean, true, it's got a bit of a something here and there, but really, it's cool. The critter is cool, the fact that we have no idea of where did they "come from" and/or have been all these years is cool… there is a bit of a something:

"They are big, mean… like most monsters I guess."

… excuse me? You used to seeing monsters, chief? Why hasn't the Foundation interrogated the shit out of these people? No matter who they are, if they have anomalous intel they are people of interest and have to be interviewed further, and if they don't cooperate, the Foundies would want to crack them open, then amnesticise them… or hire them at least.

Anyhow, +1.

Edit: Got it, and I understand the image issue perfectly. Kind of fitting in this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, I saw this on chat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the description of the thing sounds utterly ADORABLE

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Here it is, a rewrite of an earlier article posted by Vortex_Wakiwu (that ended up being a pretty extensive rewrite). Thanks to everybody who provided feedback in chat. Hope you like it!
